text: The surgeon begins addressing an incarcerated right indirect inguinal hernia at the medial umbilical ligament, progressing laterally towards Bogros space. The peritoneal flap appears thin, requiring careful manipulation near the infraepigastric vessel to avoid sticking, while pushing fat over the nerves.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to take down the peritoneal flap in a standard fashion, with a monopolar scissor and camera also visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects into the loose areolar plane within the supravesicular space, aiming to create a large flap for accommodating an extra-large Bard 3D Max mesh.

The surgeon pulls the indirect hernia sac medially while applying cautery, gently sweeping the sac away from the spermatic cord, which falls away. A small cord lipoma is visible, left with the cord initially. The hernia sac is manipulated to reveal vessels coming into view, with the surgeon maintaining a position medial to the vas deferens.
The surgeon continues the medial dissection, pulling up on the indirect hernia sac while applying cautery. The vas deferens is visible, falling laterally as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon uses an oblique camera angle to continue dissection, pulling up on the flap and suturing it to prevent buckling. The dissection extends below the iliac down to the obturator fossa, ensuring the mesh can lay flat. The chronomortis vein is visible to the right.

The surgeon continues the dissection around the corner, removing tissue medial to the vas deferens. The deferential vessels are visible, and care is taken to keep them intact. The lateral dissection plane is widened to ensure the mesh will lay flat without buckling during closure.
The surgeon places an extra-large Bard 3D Max mesh over the indirect hernia site, ensuring lateral fixation to prevent buckling and achieve proper placement.

The surgeon drapes the mesh across the iliac vessels, positioning it around the corner and fixating it laterally on Cooper's ligament, just medial to the iliac vessels. Two fixations are applied to ensure the mesh lays flat, followed by a high medial fixation.

The surgeon demonstrates the complete separation of the flaps from the mesh, ensuring the mesh lays flat. The superior aspect of the mesh is positioned snugly to prevent upward migration once desufflation occurs.
The surgeon closes the flap over the indirect space, ensuring there are no visible gaps. The assistant, not visible in the frame, aids in collapsing the space to prevent air and CO₂ entrapment.
The surgeon sutures the flap to close down the space around the indirect sac, aiming to prevent the formation of an internal hernia. The indirect sac is manipulated to reduce the risk of recurrence.
